title: Missing Meet Extension
content: Traditions first  For the last 5 years, I have worked primarily remotely. For the last 3 (almost there), I’ve been working at Contentsquare with headquarters in Paris. There are some coworkers in Berlin, but my main team fellows work from different cities: Paris, Lyon, Nice, Orléans, and Pula.  It happens that a few times per year, we meet for an offsite together — a company event for the whole Mobile SDK team. We meet, discuss problems shoulder to shoulder, and find ways to solve them. And bond, of course, by repeating old traditions and grounding new ones.  What would we be without traditions? Probably still sitting in caves, eating berries, and scared of lightning.  Since we don’t see each other so often, it feels natural to have something that reminds us of that fun time together during the offsite. So I founded another tradition in our team — simple and obvious. We play the Secret Santa with dice game. There you have it all: joy, drama, envy, and a bit of anger.  Simple rules:    limited budget (10–15 euros)   a single gift from each person   must be fun or silly     Silliness is the base  We don’t meet during Christmas. It often happens 1–3 months after it. But that doesn’t stop us from doing it. In fact, having Secret Santa in February sounds exactly in the spirit of the tradition — being a bit silly 🤪  I won’t describe all the gifts we had during the last game, but one of them was a Bullshit Button.    It’s a very simple device with a single purpose. When you hit the button, it plays a random sound clip with the word “bullshit”. That’s it.  Purpose? To express yourself during meetings with your fellows when you hear some nonsense or “very” important information.  It wouldn’t be true if I said that everyone wanted to win this button during the game. But there were some battles for it, for sure 😈  Time takes it all  Many rains have passed since that game. We’ve had some Flutter Fellow calls where we asked Florian (who won the button) to use his prize for its intended purpose. But to be honest, it doesn’t sound or look that effective during video meetings 😐  So eventually, we stopped requesting to use the button.  Tiny tiny lightning  Last Wednesday, at the end of the day, after some calls, I had a flashback about this button and its purpose…  I quickly checked the internet and was surprised by the results.     There was still no Chrome (or Mozilla) extension for Google Meet calls with the Bullshit Button.   That sounded unforgivably wrong to me.  So I did what I must: vibe-coded a new extension 😅  Very Important extension  So what does it do in a nutshell?     Adds an additional floating button with the same name on it   Once you click on it, you’ll hear the same noise   That was it 😂  Or not? 🤔  Well, it started like that. But the experience wasn’t complete. It doesn’t make sense to express to yourself what you already know. The point is to share this experience with others.  So the next step was to add some magic, which allows you to notify other call members with the same extension about your honest feelings. Once the button is clicked, all members will hear the same noise.  Now the experience is complete!  Simple. Solidary. Silly.  No more mercy for nonsense during the two hours of “5-minute” calls.    The only downside is that if you use this button too often, it will lose its appeal and relevance. Your teammates might delete extension… and all the fun will be gone. FOREVER!  So …     Use responsibly! This extension is for entertainment purposes and expressing shared meeting fatigue. Please don’t use it to actually disrupt important discussions (even if they are full of corporate speak).   Epilogue  Our life is too serious. Sometimes we need to do not only smart things that will change this world for a better place to live (sure 😅), but we also need something fun and stupid.  Sad to admit, but after all, we’re still quite primitive monkeys who smile when someone slips on a banana.  So why should we deny the silliness inside us? No reason.  Resources  Here are some links    Chrome extension            install it and use wisely ‼️       rate it ⭐️           GitHub repo            rate it ⭐️           Bullshit sounds            well, it was just sound inspiration for me 😂

title: Dart, Flutter, and the Case of the Missing Version Number
content: The SDK Shift Or what happens when you stop painting screens and start wiring them under the hood  After many years as a mobile developer, switching to work on a mobile SDK has been… an interesting ride. At first, it feels like I’ve crossed into backend territory — but truth be told, it’s still very much the same playground. What do I mean? Well, when you’re building a mobile app, chances are you’re mostly wrangling UI. Sure, some big projects have separate teams doing just business logic, never touching a single widget. But for most mobile devs, it’s UI all day, every day.  Mobile SDKs come in all shapes and sizes. Sometimes they deal with UI — sometimes, they’re mysterious little black boxes doing secret things under the hood. That’s the case with the Contentsquare SDK. We don’t build any UI elements directly, but we do interact with them, just way deeper down in the stack.  Flutter’s Runtime Riddle Or the mystery of the missing version number  When you’re building an SDK for a framework like Flutter, there’s a good chance you’ll need to know which version of the framework the client is using. Why does it matter? Oh, let me count the ways:  📊 Analytics Clients often want analytics like app version, SDK version, and — you guessed it — framework version.  🔌 API Compatibility    Framework versions can introduce or remove APIs.   Older versions may lack features, newer ones might break stuff.   You’ll probably need to implement fallbacks.   🛠 Client Support When something breaks, clear version-aware logs help you (and them) debug faster.  👮‍♂️ Security    Some versions of the framework may have known vulnerabilities.   Your SDK might want to warn or even bail out if it detects an insecure version.   📈 Telemetry    It’s super useful to know which framework versions your clients are running on.   Helps you decide the minimum supported version based on actual usage, not gut feeling.   As you can see, knowing which framework version your client’s app is built with isn’t just nice to have — it’s survival gear for any SDK dev.   . . .  On Android and iOS, getting the OS version is easy-peasy:  val sdkVersion = android.os.Build.VERSION.SDK_INT   let systemVersion = UIDevice.current.systemVersion   Simple, right?  Now here’s the plot twist: Flutter doesn’t let you get the Flutter SDK version at runtime. That’s right — no static variable, no system call, no magical Platform.flutterVersion. Nada.  And I wasn’t the only one hoping for a better way. At some point, while deep in version-detective mode, I stumbled upon an old GitHub PR (from 2020) where someone proposed adding exactly this kind of feature — a static const with the current Flutter version.  I got my hopes up. Maybe, just maybe…  But alas, the dream was short-lived. The PR had already been closed for over a year. Rejected. Forgotten.     It felt like discovering a treasure map — only to realize the “X” was just a typo …    . . .  Yes, we still can pass the version at build time, like this:  flutter run --dart-define=FLUTTER_SDK_VERSION=$(flutter --version | head -n 1 | awk '{print $2}')   … and then read it in your app:  const String flutterVersion = String.fromEnvironment('FLUTTER_SDK_VERSION');   But here’s the catch: if you’re building an SDK, the last thing you want is to make clients jump through extra hoops. Asking them to modify their build process? That’s a big “meh” from us. So this approach was off the table.  A Glimmer of Hope Or the unexpected Dart-Flutter connection  Turns out, the tunnel isn’t completely dark — there’s a hint of flashlight ahead: we can get the Dart SDK version at runtime:  import 'dart:io';  void main() {   print(Platform.version); }   Now you might be thinking:    “How the hell does that help us?” 😈   Well, it turns out there’s a pretty solid correlation between Dart and Flutter versions. You can actually see it in Flutter’s official archive.  So, even though it’s not a one-to-one mapping, it’s close enough for practical use. And that means… we can make a Dart-to-Flutter version map:  import 'package:pub_semver/pub_semver.dart';  final Map<Version, Version> dartToFlutterVersionMap = {   Version.parse('3.8.1'): Version.parse('3.32.1'),   Version.parse('3.8.0'): Version.parse('3.32.0'),   Version.parse('3.7.2'): Version.parse('3.29.3'), };   With this map, we can guesstimate the Flutter version from the Dart version at runtime — and that’s a huge win for SDK developers.  Automating the Solution Or an introduction to a new package  I’ll be honest — for a while, our approach was pretty old-school: We kept a file with a manual Dart→Flutter version map.  Every time a new version dropped (every few weeks), we had to:     update the file   open a PR   review it   merge   release   repeat   Needless to say, that gets old fast. Not exactly what you’d call a scalable solution…  That’s when I decided to channel my inner lazy dev 🫠 — and turn the whole thing into a package:  Say hello to dart_flutter_version 🎉  What does it do? Pretty much the same thing as before, but a bit automated:     A Dart script scrapes the official Flutter docs for version info   It builds and updates the Dart→Flutter map   A GitHub Action checks the docs every night   If a new version shows up, the package auto-publishes an update to pub.dev 🚀   Now, SDKs (like ours) can depend on this package and grab the Flutter version at runtime — no extra work required from the client.  Navigating Nuances Or understanding the package’s limitations  Now, before you run off singing songs of automated glory, let’s pause for a dose of realism.  The Dart→Flutter version map is pretty reliable, but it’s not perfect. Here are a few downsides of such an approach:  🟡 Stable Channel Only  The map only covers stable Flutter releases. If you’re using beta, dev, or master, the version alignment could be quite different.  🖥 Platform Bias  The mapping is based on macOS versions from the official docs. Things might differ on Windows or Linux (we see you, brave souls).  🕐 Delay in Updates  There’s a nightly script checking for new versions, but it’s not instant. There could be a short delay between a new Flutter release and the package update.  📉 Minimum Supported Version  Only Flutter 3.0.0 and above are supported. If you’re on an older version… well, it might be time for an upgrade anyway 😅  Bottom line:  The resolved version is accurate enough for most real-world use cases — but don’t bet your life (or a CI pipeline) on it being pixel-perfect in every scenario.  The Auto-Update Advantage Or how to seamlessly stay current  And now, the cherry on top: auto-updates. 🍒  Thanks to GitHub Actions, whenever a new Dart→Flutter version pair is detected, the package:     updates the map   bumps the patch version   publishes to pub.dev   For example, here’s a snippet from the changelog:  1.0.5     Dart 3.8.1 → Flutter 3.32.1    1.0.4     Dart 3.8.0 → Flutter 3.32.0   As long as SDK developers use the ^ symbol in their pubspec.yaml:  dart_flutter_version: ^1.0.5   they will automatically get the latest version the next time they run:  flutter pub get   This is especially useful in SDKs like ours, where clients might install the package today, next week, or in six months — and we want to make sure they are always getting the freshest version info, without lifting a finger 💅  The Sequel Nobody Asked For Or how a comment brought a PR back from the grave  Just when I thought the case was closed — literally — a little plot twist came along.  After publishing the shiny new dart_flutter_version package, I couldn’t help myself and dropped a message in that same old GitHub PR thread. I included a link to the package and a bit of context, just for closure, really.  And then… something unexpected happened 😏  Within a month, the thread was reopened. A few months later — boom 💥 — the PR was merged (May 21). That long-awaited static variable? It’s finally becoming a reality.  This is a huge win for the Flutter community — and, ironically, the beginning of the end for my humble package 😅  But hey, it’ll still be useful for a while. Not everyone updates Flutter overnight — some apps take months (or years 😬) to catch up. So until the dust settles and the new version becomes the norm:     Live long and prosper, dart_flutter_version 🖖   You did your duty, sir 🫡
